narrative: Dear [Student's Name], I know this rejection letter stings, especially after all the effort you've put into your law school dreams, and I want to walk you through why our predictive tool saw your path to the bar exam as a tough one—76% chance of not passing, based on patterns from thousands of students like you. Picture your story starting with those early days in law school: your Year 1 grades landed you in the bottom decile, the very first and strongest signal that set off alarms, as it often foreshadows struggles to build momentum. That low LSAT score of 31 piled on next, whispering that the raw analytical firepower needed for bar-level reasoning might be hard to muster. By Year 3, another bottom-decile finish reinforced this, showing the climb didn't happen even after time to adjust. Your upper-middle family income, while a solid foundation, surprisingly nudged the outlook downward in our lens, perhaps because it correlates with profiles that haven't historically overcome these academic hurdles. A 2.7 undergrad GPA added to the weight, painting a picture of consistent challenges in rigorous settings. Being a white male in full-time study rounded out the edges, each subtly tilting toward the risks we've seen play out before. It's not that any one thing doomed you—it's this whole mosaic, like a house of cards where the base wobbles first. You've got target_law drive shining through at full strength, which is huge, but the model weighs the headwinds heaviest. This isn't the end of your story; many have rewritten predictions like yours with targeted prep. What if you shored up those early weaknesses now? Hang in there—you're more than these numbers.
